caption.text: Imagine Clarendon Hill before it became a defined residential neighborhood, a landscape shaped by intersecting rail lines, freight corridors, and dispersed industrial activity that extended across the edges of Somerville. In the late 19th century, this was not a settled community in the way we understand it today, but an active industrial frontier tied closely to the economic expansion of Boston. Movement, extraction, and production structured daily life here, with land use dictated less by residential planning and more by the demands of industry and regional connectivity.
caption.title: Introduction

caption.text: At the heart of this landscape were rail corridors that functioned as the primary organizing force of the region. These lines carried raw materials into factories, distributed finished goods outward, and moved workers between home and industrial sites. In doing so, they embedded Clarendon Hill within a dense regional system of manufacturing, shipbuilding, and freight-based exchange, linking Somerville directly to Boston’s industrial economy and beyond.
caption.title: 1892 map of West End's System

caption.text: On the ground, industry actively reshaped the physical environment itself. Brick manufacturing operations extracted clay from the land and transformed it into building materials that would literally construct the expanding city. These sites were labor-intensive and heavily reliant on immigrant workers, whose presence not only fueled production but also laid the demographic and social foundations for future residential development in the area.
caption.title: Tuft’s Brick Manufacturing Company

caption.text: Even institutions like Tufts University were deeply embedded within this industrial ecosystem. Before its evolution into a modern academic institution, Tufts was connected to local brick manufacturing and regional construction networks. Education, labor, and industry existed in proximity, reflecting a landscape where knowledge production and material production were not yet separate spheres.
caption.title: Tuft’s University

caption.text: Other industrial factories took their place amongst the expanding landscape, such as the Middlesex Bleachery and Dye Works, providing numerous avenues of work for immigrants coming to Boston.
caption.title: Middlesex Bleachery and Dye Works

caption.text: Beneath this visible world of factories and rail lines, critical infrastructure systems operated continuously to sustain growth. Pumping stations supplied water to industry, managed waste, and ensured that densely populated and heavily industrialized areas could function. These systems were essential but often invisible, forming the hidden support structure of urban life.
caption.title: Mystic Waterworks

caption.text: At a larger regional scale, the Mystic River and its reservoir system were engineered into the urban fabric. Rather than existing as a natural boundary, the river was increasingly controlled and redirected to serve the needs of expanding industry, residential development, and institutions like Tufts College. Water became a managed resource, deeply integrated into the logic of urban expansion.
caption.title: Mystic River Reservoir

caption.text: Even earlier hydrological systems, like the Millers River and its surrounding basins, reveal a landscape that once functioned as a complex network of waterways. Before being filled, buried, or redirected, these drainage systems shaped where development could occur. What appears today as solid urban land once existed as a fluid, shifting environmental system that guided early settlement patterns.
caption.title: Cambridge, Somerville, and Charlestown drainage areas of Millers River and Basins

caption.text: By the late 19th century, this transformation was fully visible. The Mystic River had become a working landscape where water, infrastructure, and urban development were tightly interconnected. Factories relied on it, housing grew around it, and institutions like Tufts expanded within the same system, demonstrating how natural and built environments had become inseparable.
caption.title: Mystic Reservoir 1898

caption.text: Today, remnants of this system still stand in altered form. The Mystic Waterworks, once central to industrial water supply, no longer functions in its original capacity but remains physically present within the landscape. Its reuse and preservation reflect a broader shift from infrastructure of production to infrastructure of memory and adaptive reuse.
caption.title: Current Photograph of Mystic Waterworks

caption.text: Historically, the Mystic Waterworks served as a stabilizing core of the entire system. It regulated water distribution across factories, rail corridors, and residential areas, making dense urban development possible. Without it, the scale and intensity of industrial growth in Somerville would not have been sustainable.
caption.title: Mystic Waterworks

caption.text: Taken together, railways, water systems, and industrial sites formed a tightly interdependent network. Rail enabled industrial expansion, industry increased demand for water, and water systems supported both processes. Each component reinforced the others, producing a self-sustaining system of urban growth across Clarendon Hill and the wider Somerville region.
caption.title: Railway Development

caption.text: Over time, this system began to shift from industrial organization toward human mobility. The West End Street Railway introduced electrified streetcars that connected Somerville more directly to Boston, allowing residents to live farther from industrial centers. This change marked an early transition toward residential expansion and commuter-based development.
caption.title: West End Street Railway Co.

caption.text: In earlier maps, Elm Street appears as a quieter, less developed edge of the network. Before becoming a transit-linked location, it existed on the periphery of major rail systems. Its later transformation into a connectivity hub illustrates how transportation infrastructure actively reshaped land use and urban identity over time.
caption.title: Elm Street Station

caption.text: At the same time, new forms of personal mobility were emerging. Bicycle advertisements from the 1890s reflect a growing culture of independence, leisure, and urban exploration. Cycling allowed residents to navigate beyond fixed rail lines, expanding how people experienced and moved through the city.
caption.title: Bicycle Advertisement

caption.text: Today, that legacy continues through the MBTA system. Modern transit no longer serves industrial production, but instead supports residential life, education, and neighborhood connectivity, reinforcing Somerville as part of a broader commuter and community network.
caption.title: West Somerville Station

caption.text: Alongside physical infrastructure, social infrastructure also developed in response to urban growth. The Cambridge Almshouse provided housing, care, and medical isolation for vulnerable populations, including during outbreaks such as smallpox. It reflects how cities were forced to address the social consequences of rapid industrialization.
caption.title: Community Infrastructure

caption.text: Today, this same site has been transformed into an international school, shifting from local welfare provision to global education. This change reflects a broader redefinition of institutional space, from managing survival to supporting learning and global connection.
caption.title: Almshouse

caption.text: Religious and charitable institutions like the Little Sisters of the Poor also existed within this industrial landscape. They provided long-term care for the elderly, offering stability within a rapidly changing and often unstable urban environment.
caption.title: Little Sisters of the Poor

caption.text: These spaces of care remind us that alongside factories and infrastructure, systems of compassion and support were also embedded in the urban fabric.
caption.title: Little Sisters of the Poor

caption.text: Healthcare infrastructure expanded as population density increased, with institutions like Somerville Hospital becoming essential to public health in an industrializing city.
caption.title: Somerville Hospital

caption.text: Comparing earlier maps reveals just how rapidly the area developed, with open or lightly built land transforming into dense urban infrastructure within a few decades.
caption.title: Somerville Hospital

caption.text: As the city grew, the hospital became a central institution in daily life, reflecting the increasing need for accessible and organized medical care.
caption.title: Somerville Hospital

caption.text: With increased mobility and population movement, commercial spaces such as hotels emerged to support travelers, workers, and transient populations moving through Somerville.
caption.title: Woodbridge Hotel

caption.text: These hotels reflect the growth of service economies that developed alongside industrial and residential expansion.
caption.title: Woodhouse Hotel

caption.text: By the mid-20th century, Clarendon Hill itself underwent major transformation as public housing replaced earlier industrial land use. This shift marked an effort to stabilize housing and support working-class communities.
caption.title: Public Housing Project

caption.text: However, this residential stability was disrupted by highway construction. The development of Interstate 93 physically divided neighborhoods and reoriented regional infrastructure priorities.
caption.title: Construction of I-93

caption.text: Community members actively resisted these changes, expressing concerns about displacement, fragmentation, and the loss of neighborhood cohesion.
caption.title: Push Back

caption.text: Protests became a visible form of resistance, challenging the assumption that large-scale infrastructure equaled progress.
caption.title: "Bury I-93"

caption.text: Organized marches reflected coordinated community action demanding that infrastructure serve residents rather than disrupt them.
caption.title: Poster of a March to Protest the Building of I-93 June 17th

caption.text: Families, especially mothers, were central to this resistance, emphasizing the lived impact of infrastructure decisions on everyday life.
caption.title: Mothers Protesting at Construction Site of I-93

caption.text: Over time, increased traffic and regional infrastructure development fragmented local cohesion, shifting Clarendon Hill into a more divided urban landscape.
caption.title: Division of Community

caption.text: Despite these disruptions, redevelopment continued across Somerville, reshaping dense urban neighborhoods in response to new planning priorities.
caption.title: Union Square Getting a New Look, Aug. 14, 1980

caption.text: Urban planning remained active and ongoing, with each new project continuing to reshape the built environment.
caption.title: Urban Development

caption.text: Today, redevelopment efforts in Clarendon Hill continue to evolve, building new housing while engaging with the layered history of the site.
caption.title: Current Development in Clarendon Hill

caption.text: At this pivotal moment in 1895, overlapping systems of rail, water, and industry define the landscape. These interconnected infrastructures form the foundation of the neighborhood’s transformation into the residential and community-centered space that exists today.
caption.title: Mystic River & Clarendon Hill Today
